coredata.dc:title: The mesoporous silica nanoparticle@carbon dots-Eu3+ based ratiometric fluorescence probe for the point-of-care testing of tetracycline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jietong
Cui, Xipeng
Zhong, Shuhua
He, Yu
Song, Gongwu
coredata.dc:description: The development of a sensitive and facile method for the detection of tetracycline (TC) is crucial for food safety and environmental protection, as TC residues pose a major threat to human health and the ecological environment. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence probe containing mesoporous silica nanoparticle (MSNs) encapsulated CDs (MSNs@CDs) and Eu3+ is constructed for the detection of TC. With the addition of TC into MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ solution, the internal filter effect (IFE) of the TC to MSNs@CDs induces the quenching of the blue fluorescence emission at 430 nm of the MSNs@CDs, while the antenna effect (AE) of Eu3+ to β-diketone structure of TC amplifies the red fluorescence emission at 617 nm of the Eu3+. Therefore, based on the ratio changes of the two fluorescence signals (I616/I430), a sensitive and selective method for the detection of TC is established. The detection range of this method is from 0 to 50 μM with detection limit of 24 nM. Since there are obvious color changes throughout the TC detection process, a 3D-printing device based on the smartphone is designed for digital image detection of TC. The picture of solution is taken by smartphone and the color signal is converted into digital signal by color recognition software, thus realizing the visual and rapid quantitative detection of TC. The linear regression equation is R/(G + B) = 0.01CTC+0.49, and the detection limit is 40 nM. This method is applied for the detection of TC in the real sample with recoveries from 93.58 % to 108.1 %, which provides a new strategy for rapid and on-site detection of TC in actual water samples for environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MSNs@CDs
Ratiometric fluorescence
Digital image detection tetracycline
Portable device

Smartphone-based device combined with MSNs@CDs-Eu probe for the detection of TC
The detection of TC in real samples
Feasibility of TC detection based on MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe
The detection of TC based on MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ ratiometric fluorescence probe
Smartphone-based device combined with MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe for the detection of TC
The selectivity of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe for the detection of TC
The detection of TC by MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe in actual samples

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM spectra of MSNs before calcination (a), after calcination (b), and MSNs@CDs (c, d).
(a) XRD spectra, (b) FTIR spectra of MSNs before and after calcination and MSNs@CDs; (c) EDS spectrum of MSNs@CDs; (d)XPS full spectrum of MSNs and MSNs@CDs

(a)The fluorescence spectra of MSNs@CDs (black curve), MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ (red curve), MSNs@CDs-Eu3++TC (green curve), MSNs@CDs + TC (blue curve); (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of TC and fluorescence spectra of MSNs@CDs. The concentration of MSNs@CDs is 50 mg/L. The concentration of Eu3+ is 85 μM. The concentration of TC is 10 μM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ solution upon addition of 0–50 μM TC; (b) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I616/I430) of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe and TC concentration in 0–50 μM; (c) CIE chromaticity diagram for TC analysis by MSNs@CDs-Eu3+; (d) The linear relationship between the ratio of R/(G + B) and the concentration of TC in the solution of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe. Inset: the color change of the MSNs@CDs-Eu3+solution with different TC concentrations under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) The influence of different interfering substances on the detection of TC by MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe, (b) the color of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe in the presence of different interfering substances under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp.

Synthesis of MSNs@CDs and principal diagram of TC detection by MSNs@CDs-Eu3+. (A ratiometric fluorescence probe containing MSNs@CDs and Eu3+ is constructed for the detection of TC with visual color change. Then, a 3D-printing device based on the smartphone is designed for digital image detection of TC.)
Comparison of different fluorescence materials for the detection of TC.

head.ce:title: The mesoporous silica nanoparticle@carbon dots-Eu3+ based ratiometric fluorescence probe for the point-of-care testing of tetracycline

College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ezhou Ecological and Environmental Monitoring Center for Department of Ecology and Environment of Hubei Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ezhou Ecological and Environmental Monitoring Center for Department of Ecology and Environment of Hubei Province，Ezhou 436000，China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of a sensitive and facile method for the detection of tetracycline (TC) is crucial for food safety and environmental protection, as TC residues pose a major threat to human health and the ecological environment. In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence probe containing mesoporous silica nanoparticle (MSNs) encapsulated CDs (MSNs@CDs) and Eu3+ is constructed for the detection of TC. With the addition of TC into MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ solution, the internal filter effect (IFE) of the TC to MSNs@CDs induces the quenching of the blue fluorescence emission at 430 nm of the MSNs@CDs, while the antenna effect (AE) of Eu3+ to β-diketone structure of TC amplifies the red fluorescence emission at 617 nm of the Eu3+. Therefore, based on the ratio changes of the two fluorescence signals (I616/I430), a sensitive and selective method for the detection of TC is established. The detection range of this method is from 0 to 50 μM with detection limit of 24 nM. Since there are obvious color changes throughout the TC detection process, a 3D-printing device based on the smartphone is designed for digital image detection of TC. The picture of solution is taken by smartphone and the color signal is converted into digital signal by color recognition software, thus realizing the visual and rapid quantitative detection of TC. The linear regression equation is R/(G + B) = 0.01CTC+0.49, and the detection limit is 40 nM. This method is applied for the detection of TC in the real sample with recoveries from 93.58 % to 108.1 %, which provides a new strategy for rapid and on-site detection of TC in actual water samples for environmental protection.

A ratiometric fluorescence probe containing MSNs@CDs and Eu3+ is constructed for the detection of TC with visual color change. Then, a 3D-printing device based on the smartphone is designed for digital image detection of TC.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are usually as medicine to treat infectious diseases for humans or dairy animals. Tetracycline (TC), which acts effectively against both Gram-positive as well as Gram-negative bacteria, is a very important antibiotic and widely used as an animal medicine and food additive [1]. The TC has the advantages such as broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, minimal side effects [2] and low cost [3]. However, due to the misuse and improper treatment of TC, its residue in the aquatic environment exceeds the standard, causing serious environmental pollution [4,5]. TC is difficult to degrade and accumulates in the food chain, seriously damaging human health and increasing the risk of certain problems such as joint diseases, liver toxicity, endocrine disorders, and central nervous system defects [6,7]. In addition, the enrichment of TC residues in the environment can cause bacterial resistance, which is a major public health threat worldwide [8]. In order to control and govern TC pollution, many countries have set TC maximum residue limits in the products. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore accurate and efficient method for the analysis of TC residues in water, which is beneficial for the protection of environment, food safety and public health.

There are a variety of methods developed for the detection of TC, including ultra-high performance liquid chromatography tandem-mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) [9], electrochemical analysis [10], capillary electrophoresis mass spectrometry (CE-MS) [11], colorimetry [12], and fluorescence analysis [13–15]. Among them, fluorescent analysis and colorimetry are considered most promising for their high sensitivity, simple operation, fast response, and low-cost. The development of advanced fluorescence materials offers more choice for the construction of fluorescence probe. A lot of emerging materials such as metal organic framework (MOF) [16], carbon dots (CDs) [17], nanoclusters (NCs) [18], and quantum dots (QDs) [19] are employed for the detection of TC. Especially, CDs-based fluorescence probes have attracted great interest for their simple synthesis, low toxicity, and good optical properties, which have played an increasingly important role in the field of analysis.

Due to the formation of stable complexes between the β-diketone structure of TC and Eu3+, a new red fluorescence emission at 617 nm is produced, which could be utilized to establish ratiometric fluorescence probe with advanced fluorescence materials for the detection of TC [20,21]. The ratiometric fluorescence probes contain fluorescent signals of two different wavelengths synchronously [22]. Therefore, the external interference is eliminated by providing the inherent self-calibration ability and significantly improve their color variation range and detection sensitivity [23]. The visible color change from fluorescence could combine with digital image colorimetry (DIC) to develop the point-of-care testing (POCT) [24–27]. The image of fluorescent color is acquired via the camera of smartphone, and the signals are transferred into RGB values (red, green and blue) with the APP of smartphone, which represent the concentrations of the analytes. Unfortunately, most of ratiometric fluorescence probe based on nanoparticles is very sensitive to the environment such as harsh pH or high salt. In addition, the dispersity and stability of the probe should be improved. Hence, it still remains a big challenging to obtain highly stable and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of TCs in a complex environment.

Mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) are a class of porous nanomaterials with stable backbone, large specific surface area, low toxicity, tunable pore size, and environment friendly, which can be used to encapsulate some nanoparticles, thus creating functional composites with excellent quality [28–30]. The strong adsorption capacity of MSNs can also be used as an adsorbent to successfully remove pollutants in the water environment [31–33]. At present, many studies have reported that CDs are encapsulated in the pore structure of MSNs. Since silicon is considered optically transparent, MSNs will not interfere with the fluorescence performance of CDs [34]. Therefore, the synthesized nanocomposites not only have high fluorescence intensity, but also enhance their fluorescence stability. There are more and more studies on their application in chemical sensing. Wang et al. developed a fluorescent nanomaterial sensor based on MSNs and QDs composite, and applied it to the detection of heavy metal ions Hg2+ [35]. Bhogal et al. prepared MSNs-based molecular imprinting polymers coated CDs and used them as fluorescent probes to determine triclosan in water samples in a more efficient and sensitive way [36]. Therefore, MSNs can be used as an ideal support matrix to improve the stability and optical performances of fluorescent probes.

In this work, MSNs encapsulated CDs (MSNs@CDs) and Eu3+ were employed to construct the composite of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ for ratiometric fluorescence detection of TC (Scheme 1 ). When TC was added into MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ solution, TC replaces the water molecules around Eu3+ to form stable complexes between the β-diketone structure of TC and Eu3+, and the red fluorescence of Eu3+ at 617 nm is restored through the antenna effect (AE). Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of CDs at 430 nm are quenched at the same time due to the internal filtration effect (IFE) between MSNs@CDs and complexes of Eu3+-TC, allowing sensitive detection of TC based on the ratiometric fluorescence method. Since the solution is accompanied by obvious fluorescence color changes (from blue to red) with the addition of TC, a 3D-printing device based on the smartphone is designed for the DIC of TC. The picture of solution is taken by smartphone. Then, the color signal is converted into digital signal by color recognition software, thus realizing the visual and rapid quantitative detection of TC. MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ is used for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection of TCs in water sample, which provides a new method for the POCT of TC. The POCT for TC aim to deliver results quickly, be user-friendly, and exhibit high specificity and sensitivity, which plays a crucial and provides rapid and reliable detection of tetracycline residues at the point of need. It is significant to enable timely decision-making in various settings for the detection of TC, including food safety inspections and environmental monitoring.

Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O), tetracycline (TC), amoxicillin (AMX), chloramphenicol (CHL), roxithromycin (ROX), kanamycin (KAN), glycine (Gly), glutamic (Glu), leucine (Leu), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), MnCl2, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (analysis standard), ether (analysis standard), MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, ZnCl2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KCl, NaCl, AlCl3 were purchased from Tianjin Baishi Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Tryptophan (Try) and arginine (Arg) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonia solution was purchased from Kaifeng Dongda Chemical Co. Ltd. (Kaifeng, China).

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was performed using a Zeiss Sigma500 SEM (OPTON, China). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed using Talos-200 FEI HRTEM (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The surface area and pore size analyzer (BET) were performed using QDS-MP-30 (Quantachrome, America). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were carried out on a spectrum one FT-IR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, America). Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) was performed using a Bruker Flash6|30 EDS (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed with a VGEscalab 200 spectrometer using an aluminum anode (AlKα) with a background pressure of 2 × 10−9 mbar (Thermo Fisher, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded on a D8-advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The UV–vis absorption spectra were analyzed by a Lambda 35 UV spectrometer (PerkinElmer, America). Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetimes were studied by a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, America) and a FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Britain)，respectively.

The synthesis of MSN was based on a modification of previous methods [37,38]. Typically, 0.5g CTAB, 10 mL ethanol and 0.8 mL ammonia solution were dissolved and mixed in 70 mL deionized water for 30 min. Then, 2.5 mL TEOS was added into the microemulsion system and stirred at room temperature and for 16 h in dark. The precipitated solid products were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 8 min and washed with deionized water and ethanol three times. Then, the CTAB was removed by calcination for 3 h in the air atmosphere of 550 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the white solid MSNs was obtained.

First, 75 mg of MSNs were added to 10 mL of ethanol and treated with ultrasound for 5 min to disperse evenly. Then 1.742 g arginine was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water completely. The two solutions were homogeneously mixed and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. Then the products were acquired by cooling down, centrifuging, washing and dried in a vacuum oven at 65 °C for 8 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe for the detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: 300 μL of MSNs@CDs (500 mg/L) and 50 μL of EuCl3 (5 mM) were added and mixed into 2700 μL of deionized water to form the MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe. Different concentrations of TC were added into the probe and the fluorescence emission the solutions were recorded at 430 nm and 616 nm under excitation of 360 nm after incubation for 4 min at room temperature. Based on the relationship between the intensity ratio of two different fluorescent signals (I616/I430) and TC concentration, the corresponding linear regression equations were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based device combined with MSNs@CDs-Eu probe for the detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of TC were dropped into the MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe and placed the cuvette into the sample tank of the POTC device. Under irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, the fluorescence images of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe with different concentrations of TC solution were obtained by smartphone. The corresponding RGB (Red, Green, Blue) values for each image were readout by the color recognition software of smartphone. The R/(G + B) values against the concentration of TC were analyzed to establish the linear relationship for the detection of TC.

To explore the selectivity of the MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe, some possible interferences were selected to evaluate the anti-interference properties. TC was replaced by the same concentration of interfering substances such as KAN, CHL, AMX, ROX, Gly, Cys, Glu, His, Try, Leu, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, etc. The fluorescence intensity at 430 nm and 616 nm were measured under the same conditions and the effect on the ratio of the two fluorescence signals was recorded to evaluate the selectivity of the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the practicability of the MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe for TC detection, a variety of local water samples (Sand lakes water, East-lake water, Yangtze River water, Tap water) were collected for testing. The water samples were centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min to remove large impurities, and then a 0.22 μm filter membrane was used to remove small impurities.

MSNs@CDs composites were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using MSNs as the supporter and arginine as the nitrogen source. The structures and morphologies of MSNs and MSNs@CDs are characterized by TEM and SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, MSNs is a spherical dendritic mesoporous structure with an average diameter of about 150 ± 10 nm, and there is no significant change in morphology of MSNs before and after calcination (Fig. 1a and b). After the formation of MSNs@CDs, the rough surfaces, and contrast of dark vs. pale parts in the MSNs@CDs indicates CDs has successfully filled into the mesoporous structure of MSNs (Fig. 1c and d).

In the spectra of XRD pattern of MSNs, CDs and MSNs@CDs (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1), the MSNs have typical broad envelope peaks from 14° to 36° and no sharp diffraction peaks before and after calcination, which indicate that MSNs is amorphous structure. The CDs exhibit a strong diffraction peak at 21.6° in the XRD spectra, which is assigned to the (002) crystalline plane of graphite (Fig. S1). There is no significant change of XRD pattern from MSNs and that of MSNs@CDs (Fig. 2a). The surface functional groups of MSNs and MSNs@CDs are further analyzed by FTIR (Fig. 2b). The characteristic peak at 1084 cm−1 belongs to the asymmetric tensile vibration of Si–O–Si. The absorption peaks at 459 cm−1 and 806 cm−1 belongs to the bending vibration of Si–O and the symmetric stretching vibration of Si–O–Si, respectively. The absorption peak at 966 cm−1 is caused by the bending vibration absorption of Si–OH. These characteristic absorption peaks prove that MSNs are successfully synthesized (black curve, Fig. 2b). In addition, the characteristic absorption peaks at 2852 cm−1 and 2924 cm−1 belong to the symmetric and asymmetric tensile vibration of C–CH2 on CTAB, respectively. After calcination, the characteristic absorption peaks of CTAB disappear, indicating that CTAB is successfully removed and MSNs were obtained (red curve, Fig. 2b). The FTIR spectra of the show MSNs@CDs composites have no significant changes compared with that of MSNs because the CDs exist in the mesoporous structure of MSNs (blue curve, Fig. 2b).

The EDS spectra of MSNs@CDs show the presence of C, Si, O, N elements which are the main elements in the MSNs@CDs, while the EDS spectra of MSNs only have C, Si, O elements (Fig. 2c and Fig. S2), which demonstrate the introduction of CDs into mesoporous structure of MSNs. Fig. 2d shows the XPS full-scan spectrum of MSNs and MSNs@CDs. There are four major peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV, 533 eV and 104 eV, belonging to C1s, N1s, O1s and Si2p, respectively. Further analysis of the high-resolution XPS spectra of MSNs@CDs (Fig. S3) show that C1s at 285.0 eV, 286.5 eV and 288.9 eV can be deconvolved into three peaks attributed to C–C/CC, C–N and CO, respectively. N1s has two peaks at 399.9 eV and 400.7 eV, corresponding to CN and C–N–C, respectively. O1s has two peaks at 532.4 eV and 533.0 eV, which are attributed to CO and C–O–H, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of Si2p has a peak at 103.6 eV, corresponding to Si–O–Si.

The N2 adsorption/desorption test was applied to clarify the porosity. As shown in Fig. S4, BET was carried out to analyze the aperture structure of MSNs and MSNs@CDs. According to the N2 adsorptions-desorption isotherm of MSNs (Figs. S4a and S4c), the specific surface area of MSNs is 899.83 m2/g, the pore volume of MSNs is 1.767 cc/g with the average pore size of 3.8 nm according to Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. When CDs is encapsulated into MSNs, the specific surface area of MSNs@CDs was 70.67 m2/g (Fig. S4b). According to the pore size distribution of MSNs@CDs (Fig. S4d), the pore volume was reduced to 0.309 cc/g. It indicates that CDs has been successfully encapsulated into the channel of the MSNs.

The optical characteristic of MSNs@CDs is displayed in Fig. S5. The fluorescence emission wavelength gradually redshifts when the excitation wavelength increases from 340 nm to 380 nm, indicating that MSNs@CDs have excitation dependent fluorescence properties (Fig. S5a). The optimal excitation and emission wavelength are 350 nm and 400 nm, respectively. And the fluorescence intensity of MSNs@CDs keeps unchanged for 15 days, which indicate good stability for further use in sensing applications (Fig. S5d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of TC detection based on MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to construct a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of TC with high sensitivity, good selectivity and excellent sensing performance, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of I616/I430 and TC concentration is investigated by recording the fluorescence emission intensity at 430 nm and 616 nm. The feasibility for the detection of TC by MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ was investigated. The fluorescence emission of MSNs@CDs before and after binding to Eu3+ are almost identical (black curve and red curve in Fig. 3 a). When only TC is added into MSNs@CDs, the fluorescence emission of MSNs@CDs at 430 nm decreases, and a little broad peak appears from 525 nm to 650 nm (blue curve in Fig. 3a). However, in the MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe system, the addition of TC significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm and increases the fluorescence emission at 616 nm significantly (green curve in Fig. 3a). This is due to the typical β-diketone structure of TC, which can cooperate with Eu3+ and sensitize it through AE to enhance the fluorescence emission at 616 nm. The fluorescence emission at 430 nm is obviously quenching because the excitation spectra of MSNs@CDs overlap with the absorption spectra of TC (Fig. 3b), which suggest that the fluorescence quenching may be caused by IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence detection of TC can be realized through the ratio change of two fluorescence signals.

Generally, the FRET happened accompanying with the change of the fluorophore lifetime, due to energy transfer between the fluorophore and the receptor, while the fluorescence lifetime is unchanged for IFE. In order to further study the sensing mechanism for the detection of TC by MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe, the fluorescence lifetime curves of MSNs@CDs, MSNs@CDs-TC, MSNs@CDs-Eu3++TC (10 μM) and MSNs@CDs-Eu + TC (20 μM) are displayed in Fig. S6. The average fluorescence lifetimes are 4.22 ns, 4.34 ns, 3.68 ns and 3.74 ns, respectively. The addition of different concentration of TC, the fluorescence lifetimes of MSNs@CDs are almost constant, which is not agree with the principle of FRET. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of MSNs@CDs at 430 nm is due to IFE. In summary, these results reveal that the IFE induces the fluorescence quenching of MSNs@CDs and AE leads to the fluorescence enhancement of Eu3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of TC based on MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of TC, the conditions for the detection were optimized including the amount of Eu3+ in the fluorescence probe and the incubation time (Fig. S7). 50 μM of Eu3+ and 4 min of the incubation time are chosen for further experiments. With the increase of TC concentration in solution of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+, the fluorescence emission intensity of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ at 430 nm under the excitation of 360 nm decreases gradually, while the red fluorescence emission of Eu3+ is activated and gradually enhances due to AE interaction between TC and Eu3+ (Fig. 4 a). In the range of TC concentration from 0 to 50 μM, the fluorescence intensity ratio I616/I430 of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ has a good linear relationship with the concentration of TC. The linear regression equation is I616/I430 = 0.04CTC+0.13 (R2 = 0.994). The detection limit is 24 nM (Fig. 4b). The previous works for the detection of TC by other nanoprobes are cited in Table 1 and compared with our method. Our proposed method shows good detection limit and similar linear range. With help of 3D printing device, our method also gives great potential application for in-situ analysis of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based device combined with MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe for the detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 4c, the CIE chromaticity diagram of the solution color is as a function of TC concentration. The corresponding TC concentration could be detected directly by reading the color change along the direction of the line inset in Fig. 4c, which thus demonstrated the possibility for the visual detection of TC. When MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe was employed to detect different concentration of TC, the fluorescence color of probe solution showed obvious changes (In set of Fig. 4d), which is consistent with the fluorescence color changed from red area to green area in the CIE chromaticity diagram. Then, the fluorescent pictures of the probes are taken by a 3D-printing smartphone-based device, and the RGB information of the pictures are obtained for data analysis. The size information of the 3D-printing device is displayed in Fig. S8. In the range of TC concentration 5–50 μM, the R/(G + B) value has a good linear relationship with concentration of TC. The linear regression equation is R/(G + B) = 0.01CTC+0.49 (R2 = 0.998), and the detection limit is 40 nM (Fig. 4d). Therefore, a MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe-based digital image colorimetry is developed as a simple, cost-effective, sensitive, and selective technique for the analysis of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe for the detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of TC, the influence of some common ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+), amino acids (Gly, Cys, Glu, His, Try, Leu) and antibiotics (KAN, CHL, AMX, ROX) are analyzed (Fig. 5 ). The fluorescence ratio (I616/I430) of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe is measured under the same conditions by replacing TC with these interfering substances (Fig. 5a). The obvious fluorescence color change only appears when TC was added into the probe compared with the interfering substances under 365 nm lamp of our designed portable device (Fig. 5b). The results show that the ratiometric fluorescence of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe only significant influence in the presence of TC. The good selectivity and anti-interference of the system make it have the potential to detect TC in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of TC by MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the reliability of MSNs@CDs-Eu3+ probe for practical applications, the standard TC solution (1.0 μM, 5.0 μM and 10.0 μM) was spiked into the water samples (Sand lake, East lake, Yangtze river and Tap water) by standard addition method. Then our proposed method is applied to analyze the concentration of TC in the samples. The results are shown in Table S1. The recoveries were from 93.58 % to 108.1 % and relative standard deviation (RSD) were from 1.6 % to 7.7 %, which demonstrated the practicality of our method for the detection of TC in water sample.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescence probe containing MSNs@CDs and Eu3+ is constructed for the POCT of TC. After different concentration of TC is added into the probe solution, the blue fluorescence of MSNs@CDs is quenched due to IFE and the red fluorescence is enhanced due to AE between Eu3+ and TC. Since there are visible color changes during the detection of TC, a 3D-printing device based on the smartphone is design for achieving digital image detection of TC. The image of solution is taken by smartphone and the color signal is transferred into digital signal by color recognition software in smartphone, thus realizing POCT of TC. This method was applied for the detection of TC in the real water sample. The simple, rapid, reliable and visible method is proved to be on-site detection of TC in complicated sample.
This work was financially supported by national natural science foundation of China (21707030 and 82073608).

Jietong Xu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xipeng Cui: Data curation. Shuhua Zhong: Data curation. Yu He: Writing – review & editing. Gongwu Song: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Yu He reports financial support was provided by Hubei University. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2024.115804.
